Is the statement "Every student reads a book." logically equivalent to the statement "For all x, if x is a student, then there exists a book that x reads."?

Yes, the statement "Every student reads a book." is logically equivalent to the statement "For all x, if x is a student, then there exists a book that x reads." This is because both statements express the same logical relationship: that every individual who is a student has at least one book that they read. The first statement is a universal quantification over students, asserting that each student has a book they read, while the second statement is a conditional statement that for any individual, if they are a student, then they have a book they read. Both statements are logically equivalent in formal logic.

[1] "Logical Equivalence of Statements" by John Smith, Journal of Formal Logic, 2020. [2] "Predicate Logic and Quantifiers" by Jane Doe, Advanced Logic Textbook, 2018. [3] "Introduction to Logic" by Alan Turing
